stem: An insurer is pricing a product liability policy for consumer products in the US market. Historical data suggests the annual frequency of product liability claim events follows a Poisson distribution with rate λ estimated between 0.8 and 3.5 events/year. Each event causes a loss estimated between $67K and $158K (with the average loss being uncertain due to limited historical claims data). The insurer uses a risk loading factor of 19% above the expected loss. What is the fair premium, and what range of premiums is justified given the uncertainty in both frequency and severity?

ground_truth.answer: Expected loss = λ × mean severity. Range: $53.6K to $553.0K. Point estimate: $240.8K. With 19% loading: premium range [$63.8K, $658.1K]. Point estimate premium: $286.6K.

solution_steps: Expected loss = E[N] × E[X] where N ~ Poisson(λ), X = loss per event.
Note: this assumes independence between frequency and severity. In practice, high-frequency years may also have higher severity (correlation), which would widen the range further.
common_failure_modes: Using only the midpoint estimates without computing the full range
Adding frequency and severity instead of multiplying
Not including the risk loading in the premium calculation
Treating the expected loss as the premium without risk margin
Assuming frequency and severity are independent when they may be correlated
